era_context: The Classical Greek world reaches a turning point with the Battle of Mantinea, which effectively ends Theban hegemony and ushers in a period of Greek political fragmentation. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II continues to face internal challenges. The Roman Republic is consolidating power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military sophistication.

events.title: Battle of Mantinea
events.description: The Battle of Mantinea in 362 BCE is one of the most significant military engagements of the Classical Greek world. Theban forces under Epaminondas face a coalition of Greek city-states (Athens, Sparta, and others) seeking to check Theban hegemony. The battle is tactically decisive for Thebes, but Epaminondas is killed in the fighting. His death marks a turning point: Theban hegemony effectively ends, and no single Greek power emerges to replace it. The battle is often seen as marking the end of the Classical period and the beginning of the Hellenistic age, though this periodization is debated.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Mantinea is one of the best-attested events of the Classical Greek world, with contemporary accounts from Xenophon and later detailed accounts from Plutarch and Diodorus.

events.title: Death of Epaminondas
events.description: Epaminondas, the greatest Theban general and statesman of the Classical period, dies at the Battle of Mantinea. His death marks the end of an era of Theban dominance in Greek politics. Epaminondas had revolutionized Greek military tactics (the oblique phalanx) and had briefly made Thebes the leading power in Greece. With his death, no successor of comparable ability emerges, and Theban power rapidly declines. The death of Epaminondas is often seen as symbolically marking the end of the Classical Greek world.
events.certainty_note: The death of Epaminondas at Mantinea is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues to rule Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon remains the Great King of Persia, having ruled since 405 BCE. By 362 BCE, the Satraps' Revolt has been suppressed (or is in its final stages), and the Persian Empire continues to dominate the Near East. However, the empire faces ongoing challenges from internal instability and the rise of Macedon in the north.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but specific events of 362 BCE are not always clearly attested.

events.title: Roman consolidation continues in central Italy
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 362 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights. Rome is not yet a major Mediterranean power but is establishing the military and administrative structures that will enable later expansion.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 362 BCE are difficult to verify independently.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Mantinea as a 'turning point' in Greek history is sometimes overstated. While it did end Theban hegemony, it did not immediately usher in a new era; Greek politics remained fragmented for decades. The death of Epaminondas is sometimes portrayed as more symbolically significant than it was politically; Theban power had already begun to decline before his death. Some modern scholars argue that the periodization of Classical/Hellenistic is misleading and that the transition was more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: The Battle of Mantinea is one of the best-documented events of the Classical Greek world, with contemporary accounts from Xenophon. However, later sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) add details and interpretations that may not be historically accurate. The Greek sources reflect Athenian and Spartan perspectives and may underestimate Theban achievements. The significance of Epaminondas is sometimes exaggerated in later sources that emphasize individual great men. Roman and Chinese sources for this year are less detailed. The year 362 BCE is a major turning point in Greek history, but the broader implications of this turning point are debated among modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: The power vacuum left by Theban decline is filled by the rise of Macedon under Philip II
Epaminondas' death occurs at the Battle of Mantinea
The Battle of Mantinea reverses the Theban dominance established at Leuctra in 371 BCE
